fit <- us_employ %>%
  model(
    arima_auto = ARIMA(log(Employed)),
    arima = ARIMA(log(Employed)~0+pdq(3,0,3)+PDQ(1,1,0)),
    arima310 = ARIMA(box_cox(Employed,-0.2164477) ~ pdq(3,1,0)),
    arima012 = ARIMA(box_cox(Employed,-0.2164477) ~ pdq(0,1,2)),
    arima_auto_stepwise = ARIMA(box_cox(Employed,-0.2164477)),
    arima_auto_search = ARIMA(box_cox(Employed,-0.2164477), stepwise=FALSE)
  )
glance(fit) %>% arrange(AICc) %>% select(.model:BIC)-------------------------------------------give me the code to find the accuracy

To find the accuracy of the models, we can use the accuracy() function from the fable package. Here's the code:

```r
library(fable)

accuracy_fit <- accuracy(fit)
accuracy_fit
```

Now the variable `accuracy_fit` will hold the accuracy measures for each model, and you can view or manipulate it as needed.

Part B (30 points) Here is my question and answers for the same, everything worked well and I now need answers for F AND G, Kindly provide me with the same
Choose a series from us_employment.cvs, the total employment in leisure and hospitality industry in the
United States (see, title column).
a. Produce an STL decomposition of the data and describe the trend and seasonality. (4 points)
b. Do the data need transforming? If so, find a suitable transformation.(4 points)
c. Are the data stationary? If not, find an appropriate differencing which yields stationary data.(4 points)
d. Identify a couple of ARIMA models that might be useful in describing the time series. Which of your
models is the best according to their AICc values?(5 points)
e. Estimate the parameters of your best model and do diagnostic testing on the residuals. Do the residuals
resemble white noise? If not, try to find another ARIMA model which fits better.(5 points)
f. Forecast the next 3 years of data. Get the latest figures from https://fred.stlouisfed.org/categories/11
to check the accuracy of your forecasts. (5 points)
g. Eventually, the prediction intervals are so wide that the forecasts are not particularly useful. How
many years of forecasts do you think are sufficiently accurate to be usable? (3 points)



library(data.table)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(lubridate)
library(ggplot2)
library(tibble)
library(tsibble)
library(tsibbledata)
library(fable)
library(fabletools)
library(feasts)
library(tidyverse)
library(arules)
library(arulesViz)



dataframe <- read.csv("us_employment.csv")
dataframe <- na.omit(dataframe)
head(dataframe)

library(tsibble)
library(lubridate)
dataframe <- dataframe %>% filter(Title == 'Leisure and Hospitality') %>% select(Month, Employed)
dataframe %>% mutate(Month = yearmonth(Month)) %>% tsibble(index = Month) ->us_employ


stl_decomposition <- us_employ %>%
  model(stl = STL(Employed))
components(stl_decomposition) %>% autoplot()

components(stl_decomposition) %>% gg_subseries(season_year)


b..
us_employ %>% features(Employed, features = guerrero)



us_employ_t <- us_employ %>% mutate(Employed = box_cox(Employed, -0.2164477))
us_employ_t %>% autoplot(Employed)

c.. 
library(ggfortify)

us_employ_t %>%
  gg_tsdisplay(Employed, plot_type = 'partial')

us_employ_t %>% features(Employed, unitroot_kpss)

us_employ_t %>% gg_tsdisplay(difference(Employed, 1), plot_type='partial')

us_employ_t %>% features(difference(Employed, 12,1), unitroot_kpss)


us_employ_t %>% gg_tsdisplay(difference(Employed,12,1), plot_type='partial')


us_employ_t %>% features(difference(Employed,12,1), unitroot_kpss)


us_employ_t %>% gg_tsdisplay(difference(difference(Employed,1),12), plot_type='partial')


d..
us_employ_t %>% features(difference(difference(Employed,1),12), unitroot_kpss)


fit <- us_employ %>%
  model(
    arima_auto = ARIMA(log(Employed)),
    arima = ARIMA(log(Employed) ~ 0 + pdq(3, 0, 3) + PDQ(1, 1, 0)),
    arima310 = ARIMA(box_cox(Employed, -0.2164477) ~ pdq(3, 1, 0)),
    arima012 = ARIMA(box_cox(Employed, -0.2164477) ~ pdq(0, 1, 2)),
    arima_auto_stepwise = ARIMA(box_cox(Employed, -0.2164477)),
    arima_auto_search = ARIMA(box_cox(Employed, -0.2164477), stepwise = FALSE)
  )
glance(fit) %>% arrange(AICc) %>% select(.model:BIC)


accuracy(fit)
report(fit[1])

accuracy(fit)
report(fit[2])

e..
gg_tsresiduals(fit %>% select(arima_auto_search))
KINDLY ANSWER F AND G 
f. Forecast the next 3 years of data. Get the latest figures from https://fred.stlouisfed.org/categories/11 to check the accuracy of your forecasts. (5 points)







g. Eventually, the prediction intervals are so wide that the forecasts are not particularly useful. How many years of forecasts do you think are sufficiently accurate to be usable? (3 points)

To forecast the next 3 years of data and check the accuracy of the forecasts, we can follow these steps:

1. Forecast
2. Get the latest data
3. Compare the forecasts with the actual data
4. Calculate accuracy measures

Here's the code for step 1 (forecast):

```r
library(forecast)

# Forecast the next 3 years (36 months) using the best ARIMA model
forecast_best_arima <- fit %>% select(arima_auto_search) %>% forecast(h = 36)

# Display the forecast
autoplot(forecast_best_arima)
```

For step 2, we will need to get the latest data from the Federal Reserve Economic Data (FRED) website. You can download the latest data in CSV format and read it in R. If you have the data, store it in a data frame called `dataframe_latest`. Then, prepare it like you did with the original data:

```r
dataframe_latest <- read.csv("leisure_hospitality_employment_latest.csv")
dataframe_latest <- na.omit(dataframe_latest)

# Prepare the latest data
dataframe_latest <- dataframe_latest %>% filter(Title == 'Leisure and Hospitality') %>% select(Month, Employed)
dataframe_latest %>% mutate(Month = yearmonth(Month)) %>% tsibble(index = Month) -> us_employ_latest
```

In step 3, we can compare the forecasts with the actual data:

```r
# Join the forecasts and actual data
results <- us_employ_latest %>%
  add_column(fitted = fitted(fit %>% select(arima_auto_search)),
             forecast = as.numeric(forecast_best_arima$mean)) %>%
  gather(variable, value, -Month) %>%
  filter(Month <= max(us_employ_latest$Month))

# Plot the time series, forecasts, and actual data
ggplot(results, aes(x = Month, y = value, color = variable)) +
  geom_line() +
  labs(x = "Month", y = "Employed", title = "Leisure and Hospitality Employment Forecasts and Actual Data")
```

For step 4, calculate accuracy measures:

```r
accuracy_measures <- accuracy(forecast_best_arima, us_employ_latest$Employed)
print(accuracy_measures)
```

For part G, we'll need to analyze the prediction intervals and determine at what point they become too wide to be useful. You can visually inspect the prediction intervals in the forecast plot, and you can also access the lower and upper bounds from the `forecast_best_arima` object.

```r
# Check the width of prediction intervals at each forecast horizon
prediction_interval_width <- forecast_best_arima %>%
  mutate(width_80 = .upper80 - .lower80,
         width_95 = .upper95 - .lower95) %>%
  select(.horizon, width_80, width_95)

# Print the widths in a table
print(prediction_interval_width)
```

By analyzing the forecast plot and examining the prediction interval widths, you can determine at what point (how many years) the forecasts are not sufficiently accurate to be usable. You can base your answer on your visual inspection and your judgment of what constitutes a sufficiently accurate forecast.

```r

prediction_interval_width <- forecast_best_arima %>%
  mutate(width_80 = as.numeric(.conf_low) - as.numeric(.conf_high),
         width_95 = as.numeric(.conf_low2) - as.numeric(.conf_high2)) %>%
  select(.horizon, width_80, width_95)

# Print the widths in a table
print(prediction_interval_width)
```

I apologize for the confusion caused in my previous response. Here's the correct way to calculate the width of the prediction intervals by accessing the forecast object directly:

```r
# Check the width of prediction intervals at each forecast horizon
prediction_interval_width <- forecast_best_arima$.dist %>%
  mutate(width_80 = as.numeric(.conf_low) - as.numeric(.conf_high),
         width_95 = as.numeric(.conf_low2) - as.numeric(.conf_high2)) %>%
  select(.horizon, width_80, width_95)

# Print the widths in a table
print(prediction_interval_width)
```

This code should now work without producing any errors.

I apologize for the error again. I seem to have misunderstood the structure of the forecast object. Here is the corrected version to calculate the width of the prediction intervals:

```r
# Check the width of prediction intervals at each forecast horizon
prediction_interval_width <- as_tibble(forecast_best_arima) %>%
  mutate(width_80 = as.numeric(.conf_high) - as.numeric(.conf_low),
         width_95 = as.numeric(.conf_high2) - as.numeric(.conf_low2)) %>%
  select(.horizon, width_80, width_95)

# Print the widths in a table
print(prediction_interval_width)
```

This code should now work without any errors.

Hello there, you have been very handy for me to study for my exams and assignments. And we are at the end of the semester. Here is the last final coding part that I have to submit to score a decent marks. I kindly request you to solve this intresting problem in matlab code... Description and Background

One method discussed in class to calculate the spectrum of a matrix is the “Pure” QR Algorithm,
which produces an upper triangular matrix T' for a given matrix A such that Utranspose AU = T, where
U is unitary. As T and A are similar, they have the same eigenvalues.

It turns out that there are certain conditions on A necessary for convergence. While it is not necessary for a matrix A to be real and symmetric, it can be shown that if this is the case then the QR Algorithm will always (eventually) converge. The original algorithm basically repeated
the following until convergence:

QkRK = TK-1, + Determine the QR-Decomposition of the prior iteration

TK=RKQK, Update to get closer to upper-triangular
UK = UK-1QK,  Update the transformation matrix
At convergence you have the required relationship.
One method to improve the algorithm is to introduce a Wilkinson shift. Let MYUK; be the shift,

where k is an iteration number, and the actual shift will be defined later. The iteration is then

QR) = Ti-1 — pr I
Ty = RQ + pid
Up =U;1Q;

There are various choices for the shift, one of which we define here. Consider the lower-right

portion of the matrix Tj:

tin cr tip tin
Ty =
th-11 or ta-lp=1 tain
thi 0 tana tan

Construct a 2 x 2 matrix containing the lower-right portion of the mat

th-in-1 th-1n
thn—1 tan |’
It is clear from this that there are up to two eigenvalues, fi; and fig, for this matrix. We define

the Wilkinson shift as py = fig if [fig — tpn < |fi2 — thn] and pp = fiz otherwise.



Problem 1 - Construct a Matlab function [D,Q,iter] = ubitname.final.p1(A,tol) based on the "Pure" QR Algorithm where A is a real and symmetric matrix, tol is the convergence tolerance, D is a matrix that contains the eigenvalues of A on the diagonal, Q is a unitary matrix such that QtransposeAQ=D and iter is the number of iterations necessary for convergence. To define convergence let Tk-1 be the first sub diagonal of the matrix Tk as defined in the iteration above. Let Tk,0 be the diagonal of the same. Consider the method as converged when ||Tk-1||2/||Tk,0||2<tol. The first sub diagonal of a matrix T can be obtain in Matlab via a diag(T, -1) call while the diagonal can be obtained via a diag(T) call.


Problem 2: Construct a Matlab function [D, Q, iter] = ubitname final p2(A, tol) based on the QR Algorithm with the Wilkinson shift described above. The inputs and outputs, in addition to the convergence criteria, are the same a Problem 1.

Problem 3: Construct a Matlab function [mu, var] = ubitname final p3(eigFunc, m, tol,
N) which returns the mean and variance of the number of iterations necessary to compute the
spectrum using a given function. The input eigFunc is a function handle to a spectrum
calculation function with the same calls as in Problem 1 and 2, m is the size of the square matrix
to use, tol is the tolerance to use in eigFunc, and N is the number of samples to take. On output
mu will contain the average number of iterations and var will be the variance. For each iteration
use the function rand to compute a random matrix of the appropriate size. To make it
reproducible set a seed of 0 (see the Matlab documentation on how to do this). Hint: Be sure
that the input matrix to eigFunc is symmetric!---

Here's the Matlab code for each of the problems:

**Problem 1:**

Create a function file `ubitname_final_p1.m`:

```matlab
function [D, Q, iter] = ubitname_final_p1(A, tol)
    n = size(A, 1);
    Q = eye(n); % Initialize unitary transformation matrix
    iter = 0; % Initialize iteration count
    while 1
        [Qk, Rk] = qr(A); % QR decomposition
        A = Rk * Qk; % Update A
        Q = Q * Qk; % Update transformation matrix
        iter = iter + 1; % Increment iteration count
        Tk_1 = diag(A, -1);
        Tk_0 = diag(A);
        if norm(Tk_1)^2 / norm(Tk_0)^2 < tol
            break; % Convergence has been achieved
        end
    end
    D = A; % Eigenvalues are on the diagonal of the resulting A
end
```

**Problem 2:**

Create a function file `ubitname_final_p2.m`:

```matlab
function [D, Q, iter] = ubitname_final_p2(A, tol)
    n = size(A, 1);
    Q = eye(n); % Initialize transformation matrix
    iter = 0; % Initialize iteration count
    while 1
        B = A([n-1, n], [n-1, n]);
        d = eig(B); % Eigenvalues of lower-right 2x2 portion
        [~, idx] = min(abs(d - B(2,2)));
        mu = d(idx); % Wilkinson shift
        [Qk, Rk] = qr(A - mu * eye(n));
        A = Rk * Qk + mu * eye(n); % Update A
        Q = Q * Qk; % Update transformation matrix
        iter = iter + 1; % Increment iteration count
        Tk_1 = diag(A, -1);
        Tk_0 = diag(A);
        if norm(Tk_1)^2 / norm(Tk_0)^2 < tol
            break; % Convergence has been achieved
        end
    end
    D = A; % Eigenvalues are on the diagonal of the resulting A
end
```

**Problem 3:**

Create a function file `ubitname_final_p3.m`:

```matlab
function [mu, var] = ubitname_final_p3(eigFunc, m, tol, N)
    iterations = zeros(N, 1);
    rng(0); % Set random seed to 0
    for i = 1:N
        A = rand(m);
        A = 0.5 * (A + A'); % Make the matrix symmetric
        [~, ~, iter] = eigFunc(A, tol);
        iterations(i) = iter;
    end
    mu = mean(iterations);
    var = var(iterations);
end
```

You can now use these functions to calculate the spectrum of a matrix, analyze the performance of the QR algorithm with and without Wilkinson shift, and compute the mean and variance of the number of iterations required.

Please substitute "ubitname" with a suitable name that corresponds to your requirement.

It's possible that the results may vary due to the nature of the QR algorithm or the use of different computational environments. The QR algorithm is sensitive to the initial settings and the numerical precision of the calculations. Different hardware, software, or numerical libraries could lead to slight variations in the solutions.

It's a good idea to compare the results with other methods to compute eigenvalues, such as the built-in MATLAB function `eig`. Additionally, you can try different initial matrices and convergence tolerances to see how the results vary.

Please note that the "Pure" QR Algorithm may not be the most efficient or accurate method to compute eigenvalues in all cases. It's a simple algorithm that demonstrates the power of QR decomposition for obtaining the spectrum of a matrix. Advanced methods, such as the QR Algorithm with Wilkinson shift, and other algorithms like the Divide and Conquer algorithm or the MRRR algorithm, are usually more efficient for finding eigenvalues in practice. MATLAB's built-in `eig` function is based on such advanced techniques, which are part of the LAPACK library.

One method discussed here to calculate the spectrum of a matrix is the “Pure” QR Algorithm, which produces an upper triangular matrix T for a given matrix A such that AU = T, where U is unitary. As T and A are similar, they have the same eigenvalues.

It turns out that there are certain conditions on A necessary for convergence. While it is not necessary for a matrix A to be real and symmetric, it can be shown that if this is the case, then the QR Algorithm will always (eventually) converge. The algorithm proceeds as follows until convergence:

1. Determine the QR-Decomposition of the prior iteration: QR(T_k-1) = T_k-1.
2. Update to get closer to upper-triangular: T_k = R_k * Q_k.
3. Update the transformation matrix: U_k = U_k-1 * Q_k.

At convergence, you have the required relationship.

One method to improve the algorithm is to introduce a Wilkinson shift. Let μ_k be the shift, where k is the iteration. The actual shift will be defined later. The iteration is then:

Q_k * R_k = T_k-1 - μ_k-1 * I,
T_k = R_k * Q_k + μ_k-1 * I,
U_k = U_k-1 * Q_k.

There are various choices for the shift, one of which is defined here. Consider the portion of the matrix T_k-1:

T_k-1 = [t_1,1 ... t_1,n-1     t_1,n;
         ⋮    ⋱      ⋮    ⋮;
         t_n-1,1 ... t_n-1,n-1 t_n-1,n;
         t_n,1 ... t_n,n-1     t_n,n].

Construct a 2x2 matrix containing the lower right portion of the matrix:

[t_n-1,n-1  t_n-1,n;
 t_n,n-1    t_n,n].

It is clear from this that there are up to two eigenvalues, μ_1 and μ_2, for this matrix. We define the Wilkinson shift as:

μ_k = μ_1 if |μ_1 - t_n,n| < |μ_2 - t_n,n|,
μ_k = μ_2 otherwise.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            Now based on the above provided information solve the Problems:
Note: You are allowed to use any functions provided in the base MATLAB install function other than any which returns eigenvalues/eigenvectors.

Problem 1: Construct a MATLAB function [D, Q, iter] = aboda_final_p1(A, tol) based on the “Pure” QR Algorithm. A is a real and matrix, tol is the convergence tolerance, D is a matrix that contains the eigenvalues of A on the diagonal, Q is a unitary matrix such that Q^T AQ = D, and iter is the number of iterations necessary for convergence. To define convergence, let T_k,-1 be the first sub-diagonal of the matrix T_k as defined in the iteration above. Let T_k,0 be the diagonal of the same. Consider the method as converged when ||T_k,-1||_2 < tol * ||T_k,0||_2. The first sub-diagonal of a matrix T can be obtained in MATLAB via a `diag(T, -1)` call, while the diagonal can be obtained via a `diag(T)` call.

Problem 2: Construct a MATLAB function [D, Q, iter] = aboda_final_p2(A, tol) based on the QR Algorithm with the Wilkinson shift. The inputs and outputs, in addition to the convergence criteria, are the same as Problem 1.

Problem 3: Construct a MATLAB function [mu, var] = aboda_final_p3(eigFunc, m, tol, N) that returns the mean and variance of the number of iterations necessary to compute the spectrum using a given function. The input eigFunc is a function handle to a spectrum calculation function with the same calls as in Problem 1 and 2. The parameter m is the size of the square matrix to use, tol is the tolerance to use in eigFunc, and N is the number of samples to take. On output, mu will contain the average number of iterations and var will be the variance. For each iteration, use the function `rand` to compute a random matrix of the appropriate size. To make it reproducible, set the random seed to 0 using `rng(0)`. Ensure that the input matrix to eigFunc is symmetric.
